prompts: The pinhole imaging principle is based on the concept that light travels in straight lines. When light from an object passes through a small hole (pinhole) in an otherwise opaque barrier, it projects an inverted image on the opposite side. This occurs because light rays from the top, bottom, and sides of the object cross at the pinhole, continuing straight and forming an upside-down and laterally inverted image. The smaller the pinhole, the sharper the image, due to reduced light scattering, but too small a pinhole decreases brightness. This principle underlies the workings of a pinhole camera.
